chemicals.name: Because shallots belong to the Allium genus, their toxic principles are the exact same as those found in onions, garlic

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: It is described as a "very poisonous" organosulfur compound and is considered a principal toxin in Allium species58. Cats are exceptionally vulnerable to this toxin because feline hemoglobin contains eight reactive sulfhydryl groups, making their red blood cells two to three times more susceptible to oxidative damage than other species
chemicals.description: It acts as a highly reactive oxidant that causes a marked decrease in the activity of the enzyme glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) in feline red blood cells58. This decreases the concentration of glutathione, which the cells need to prevent oxidative damage58. As a result, hydrogen peroxide oxidizes the hemoglobin, causing it to denature and form clumps known as Heinz bodies on the cell mem
chemicals.name: Brief description of its mechanism of action

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: This is also classified as a "very poisonous" compound58. Notably, it has been isolated specifically from boiled Allium materials, proving that these reactive toxins easily survive high temperatures and cooking processes
chemicals.description: Similar to N-propyl disulfide, it reduces glutathione concentrations in erythrocytes58. It significantly increases methemoglobin concentrations and Heinz body counts in the blood, leading to subsequent oxidative cell damage and hemolytic anemia58. chemicals.name: Sodium n-propylthiosulfate

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: It is another primary causative agent that has been directly implicated in Allium-induced hemolytic anemia
chemicals.description: Like other Allium organosulfur compounds, it is readily absorbed through the cat's gastrointestinal tract and metabolized into highly reactive oxidants that trigger oxidative hemolysis67. Any notes on concentration or potency: It is another primary causative agent that has been directly implicated in Allium-induced hemolytic anemia
chemicals.name: Brief description of its mechanism of action

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic flavor and odor of shallots1011. Because they are present throughout the plant tissue and convert to toxins instantly upon damage, no part of the shallot is safe for a cat3.... Shallots generally have a high relative toxicity to cats, similar to onions
chemicals.description: Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides are the non-toxic parent compounds sequestered inside the plant's cells1011. When the shallot experiences mechanical trauma—such as being chewed by a cat or chopped with a knife—these precursors instantly convert into the highly reactive, cell-damaging sulfur-containing organic compounds (including alkylthiosulfonates, which are identified specifically as a primary to
chemicals.name: Alkylthiosulfonates and Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides (Precursors)

description: Shallots (Allium ascalonicum) are a member of the Allium family and severely toxic to cats. They contain the same organosulfur compounds as garlic, onions, and chives — N-propyl disulfide and related compounds that oxidise feline haemoglobin. Shallots are reportedly more potent than standard onions by weight, similar in toxicity level to garlic.
emergencyNote: Because shallots belong to the Allium genus, they cause the exact same oxidative damage and toxicosis as onions, garlic, and leeks. Since there is no specific antidote for Allium poisoning
name: Shallots
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: N-propyl disulfide and organosulfur compounds; all forms toxic — raw, cooked, pickled, and dried
Mechanism: oxidative damage creates Heinz bodies in red blood cells; haemolytic anaemia develops as affected cells are destroyed
Symptoms: lethargy, pale or yellowish gums, rapid breathing, loss of appetite, weakness, reddish-brown urine; symptoms often delayed 3–5 days
Shallots are concentrated in many sauces, dressings, and Asian dishes — never share seasoned food with cats
Contact your vet immediately if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Because shallots belong to the Allium genus
Vomiting, Diarrhea, Abdominal Pain, and Loss of Appetite
symptoms.notes: These are typically the first clinical signs of toxicosis to appear56. They occur due to direct irritation of the mouth and gastrointestinal tract and frequently result in dehydration
symptoms.onset: Usually within 1 day (24 hours) of ingestion

symptoms.name: Lethargy, Weakness, and Depression
symptoms.notes: These symptoms emerge as a secondary response to systemic hypoxia caused by the profound destruction and loss of oxygen-carrying red blood cells
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days (typically 2 to 5 days)

symptoms.name: Pale Mucous Membranes and Jaundice (Icterus)
symptoms.notes: Pale gums indicate a lack of oxygenated blood due to anemia6.... Jaundice (yellowing of the skin or mucosa) occurs because of severe red blood cell destruction (extravascular hemolysis), which leads to an excess of bilirubin in the blood (hyperbilirubinemia)
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days (typically 3 to 5 days)

symptoms.name: Tachypnea and Dyspnea (Breathing Difficulties)
symptoms.notes: This is a compensatory response as the cat's body attempts to offset the decreased blood oxygen transportation capacity and the resulting tissue hypoxia
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days

symptoms.name: Tachycardia (Rapid Heart Rate)
symptoms.notes: Tachycardia develops as the heart pumps much faster to compensate for the impaired oxygen transportation caused by the ruptured and damaged red blood cells
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days

symptoms.name: Hemoglobinuria (Dark Urine)
symptoms.notes: This is a hallmark sign of severe oxidative intravascular hemolysis714. Damaged red blood cells rupture and release hemoglobin, which is then excreted through the urinary tract7.... This process can lead to secondary acute kidney injury known as hemoglobinuric nephrosis
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days

symptoms.name: Cold Sensitivity
symptoms.notes: Affected cats may have difficulty regulating their body temperature as a systemic effect of the progressive anemia and impaired oxygen delivery to tissues

symptoms.name: Allium Breath Odor
symptoms.notes: While not harmful itself, this symptom is specifically associated with recent ingestion of Allium plants and serves as a highly valuable diagnostic clue for veterinarians evaluating the cat

treatments.name: Because shallots belong to the Allium genus, they cause the exact same oxidative damage and toxicosis as onions, garlic, and leeks. Since there is no specific antidote for Allium poisoning

treatments.description: of the procedure: Providing the cat with supplemental oxygen and transfusing whole blood to restore the body's depleted oxygen-carrying capacity34. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
treatments.notes: This is the absolute highest priority for severely affected cats presenting with life-threatening clinical signs such as respiratory distress, systemic hypoxia, or profound hemolytic anemia (specifically when the hematocrit drops below 15–20%)

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering an emetic drug under veterinary supervision to force the cat to expel the ingested shallots before the toxins are fully absorbed23. For cats, the recommended drug is an α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonist like dexmedetomidine or xylazine2.... Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This early decontamination step is highly valuable but is only indicated if the cat is currently asymptomatic and the ingestion was very recent (caught within one to two hours)23. Crucially, 3% hydrogen peroxide must never be used to induce vomiting in cats, as it is strictly contraindicated and can

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal (typically 1 to 4 g/kg) to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and gastrointestinal tract23. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is typically administered directly after inducing emesis to trap toxins before they can enter the bloodstream23. However, it should be noted that its actual efficacy in adsorbing or preventing the production of Allium-derived sulfides is currently unknown

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to rehydrate the animal, support blood pressure, and maintain proper renal perfusion34. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy (Systemic Supportive Care)
treatments.notes: This treatment is indicated if the cat presents with hypotension or has suffered extensive fluid loss from severe vomiting and diarrhea34. Furthermore, it is a critical protective measure if the cat develops hemoglobinuria (dark urine caused by ruptured red blood cells clearing through the urinary t

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering anti-sickness/anti-nausea medications to control vomiting, alongside antioxidant vitamins (such as Vitamin C and E) or N-acetyl-cysteine2.... Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Antioxidant and Gastrointestinal Medications (Symptomatic Relief)
treatments.notes: Anti-emetics and gastrointestinal protectants are utilized to manage mild-to-moderate abdominal pain and persistent vomiting210. While antioxidants are sometimes administered to combat the oxidative damage done to the red blood cells, they have shown minimal proven protective effects against Allium
